items.title: Friday's selloff was hedging, not selling — QQQ+SPY hit highest put volume day in history
items.idea: LordFed distinguishes: 'If people sell the thesis, you have a top. If people hedge the thesis, you just have a positioning problem.' Friday saw AVGO weak, GOOGL's $80B raise adding uncertainty, NFP shock (172K vs 88K). QQQ+SPY put volume hit all-time record high — institutions hedged, didn't dump. Still calling S&P 8,000.
items.angle: Cleanest framework for reading Friday's action. 8K call stands. Watch for confirmation on Tuesday open.
items.tweetA: The difference between a top and a correction in one sentence: 'If people sell the thesis, you have a top. If people hedge the thesis, you just have a positioning problem.' Friday was the highest put volume day in history for QQQ+SPY. That's hedging.
items.tweetB: 172K jobs vs 88K expected wrecked tech Friday. QQQ -4%. But the guy calling S&P 8K isn't backing down. His point: the AI trade didn't die, it just changed category. NFP gave people an excuse to trim what they were already nervous about.

items.title: 33.7% rally in 9 weeks ended with 5% Nasdaq drop — 6 of 11 sectors green even as tech crashed
items.idea: S&P ended 9-week winning streak; QQQ+SPY highest put volume day in history. All mega-caps >$1T down: AVGO -13.66%, MU deep red, TSLA -11.44%, AAPL -1.4% (best held). But 6 of 11 sectors finished green: Energy, Financials, Healthcare, Utilities, Industrials, Real Estate. Still well above 50-DMA.
items.angle: Breadth story is the real signal — 6 sectors green while tech falls 3%+ is rotation, not risk-off.
items.tweetA: S&P ended a 9-week winning streak Friday. Tech down 3%+. QQQ dropped 5% in 3 days from ATH. But here's what nobody's saying: 6 of 11 sectors finished the week GREEN. Energy, Financials, Healthcare, Utilities, Industrials, Real Estate all rose. This wasn't a market selloff. It was tech rotation.
items.tweetB: AVGO -13.66%. TSLA -11.44%. MU deep red. AAPL only -1.4% in the carnage. Tech took the hit, but the rest of the market barely noticed. After a 33.7% QQQ rally in 9 weeks — this was inevitable.

charts.desc: Trend Analyzer snapshot showing mega-cap drawdowns; QQQ vs SPY gap to 50-DMA

items.source: David Lin Report / Jay Pelosky (TPW Advisory)
items.title: The $16 trillion global spending supercycle: 'em-mification of America' and EM at 12x vs US at 22x
items.idea: Pelosky projects $10T global spending this year → $16T by 2030 across AI, climate, defense. Q1 S&P earnings expected 13% came in 23%. Key call: US multiple compression as EM reprices higher. EM at 12x vs US at 21-22x (50% discount). Long commodities, mining margins above tech margins at $4,500 gold vs $1,500 producer cost.
items.angle: The 'em-mification of America' framing — US behaves like a bad EM but trades at developed-market premium. Gap closing is multi-year.
items.tweetA: Emerging markets: 12x earnings. US: 21-22x. That's a 50% discount to buy exposure to the same $16 trillion spending supercycle. Gold miners are now generating wider margins than most tech companies. The 'rest of world' trade isn't contrarian anymore — it's just not priced in yet.
items.tweetB: Q1 S&P earnings expected: +13%. Actual: +23%. First quarter of a 3-year AI capex cycle. $16T in global defense, climate, AI spending coming by 2030. Pelosky's call: US multiple compresses, EM reprices higher. He coined it: 'em-mification of America.'

items.source: David Lin Report / Thomas Hayes (Great Hill Capital)
items.title: Alphabet's $80B equity raise — largest in US history — and 60% of Alphabet+Amazon net income is paper gains
items.idea: Alphabet and Amazon together reported $53B in 'other income'; Alphabet's $36.9B driven largely by mark-to-market gains on Anthropic. ~60% of Alphabet+Amazon combined net income was windfall, not operating improvement. Alphabet's $80B equity raise: largest in US history — Hayes reads as inability to fund capex through cash flow/debt. Defensive sectors at 25-year low (16% vs 35% historical). Alibaba as cheapest global AI play.
items.angle: If 60% of Alphabet net income is paper gains on Anthropic, what happens to earnings if Anthropic corrects? The equity raise is the tell.
items.tweetA: Alphabet just did the largest equity raise in US history — $80 billion. A company generating $80B+ in cash? No, it's a company whose capex has outpaced cash flow so badly they're now diluting shareholders. Meanwhile, $36.9B of their 'profit' was a mark-to-market gain on Anthropic. Read that twice.
items.tweetB: 60% of Alphabet and Amazon's combined net income last quarter came from paper gains on startup investments — not operations. The AI trade is real. The earnings aren't. Defensive sectors are at 25-year lows (16% of S&P vs 35% historically). Hayes says the rotation starts when a tweet ends the Iran war.

items.source: David Lin Report / Anna Wong (Bloomberg Economics)
items.title: Atlanta Fed tracking 4.3% Q2 GDP despite record-low consumer sentiment — AI capex cycle just entered year 1 of 3
items.idea: Q1 GDP 2.0%, Atlanta tracker 4.3% Q2. Consumer sentiment 48.2 record low yet consumption holds via fiscal support. Tailwinds: IEEPA tariff refunds ~$100B (+0.3pp), One Big Beautiful Bill (+0.4pp), defense spending rebuild to $1.1T (+0.6pp). AI capex: 800% growth in Google AI products, full Intel order books, capex cycles run 3yr up/3yr down — current cycle only in year 1.
items.angle: Record-low sentiment + 4.3% GDP is the defining paradox of 2026. Fiscal spending is the bridge. Warsh has no room to cut.
items.tweetA: Consumer sentiment: 48.2 — record low. Atlanta Fed GDP tracker: +4.3% in Q2. How? Tariff refunds hitting ($100B this year), defense spending exploding ($200B+ new budget), and a fiscal backstop most people aren't modeling. The economy is booming AND people feel terrible. Both are real.
items.tweetB: AI capex cycles historically run 3 years up, 3 years down. We are in year 1. 800% growth in Google AI products. Intel order books full. The next 2 years of capex are already committed. Whatever happens to sentiment — the spending is coming. Year 1 of 3.

items.source: David Lin Report / Steve Hanke (Johns Hopkins)
items.title: US deficit $955B in 7 months → $1.9T+ annual run rate; credit card 90-day delinquencies at 13.1% — highest since 2011
items.idea: Hanke: 10-year at ~4.5% is danger zone for economy with $1.9T+ annual deficit where 20%+ of federal tax revenue services interest. Credit card 90d delinquencies: 13.1% (highest since 2011). Savings rate: 2.6%. Real wages: negative. Fed switched QT→QE December, commercial banks lending ~10%/yr. Headline CPI 3.8%, nearly double target.
items.angle: 13.1% credit card delinquency is recessionary territory while GDP tracks 4.3%. This contradiction resolves sharply one way or another.
items.tweetA: Credit card 90-day delinquencies: 13.1% — highest since 2011. Savings rate: 2.6%. Real wages: negative. Yet the economy is 'booming.' Washington is running a $1.9T annual deficit and paying 20% of tax revenue just to service interest. Fiscal spending is the only thing between 4.3% GDP and recession.
items.tweetB: US deficit pace: $955B in 7 months. That's $1.9T annualized. Interest on the debt is now consuming 20%+ of all federal tax revenue. The bond vigilantes Hanke warned about? At 4.5% 10-year — they're showing up.

items.source: David Lin Report / Chris Vermeulen (The Technical Traders)
items.title: Gold: short-term $3,600/oz target (20% haircut from current), then bull flag to $8,600/oz; Bitcoin to $45K or $16K
items.idea: Vermeulen sold gold near $5,100. Short-term target $3,600/oz (20% below current), then would be major buying opportunity. Long-term bull flag points to $8,600/oz. Bitcoin: bear market, decoupled from Nasdaq, could fall to $45K or $16K. DXY: coiled spring, 5-8% move above 100 would pressure stocks and metals. S&P upside: Fibonacci near 8,500 on IPO euphoria, then blowoff top.
items.angle: $3,600 gold + $16K Bitcoin simultaneously would be the historic generational buy signal in both assets.
items.tweetA: The technician who's been right all year: Gold $3,600 short-term. Gold $8,600 long-term. He sold near $5,100. Bitcoin to $16-45K before it's over. Dollar coiled spring — if DXY breaks above 100, both stocks and metals get hit hard. He's not bearish forever. He's bearish for the next leg down.
items.tweetB: S&P bull scenario: euphoria into SpaceX/OpenAI/Anthropic IPOs drives a last melt-up to 8,500. Then a blowoff top. Gold crashes to $3,600 as real yields spike. Then you buy everything. That's the setup Vermeulen is planning around. Calendar the SpaceX IPO.

items.source: David Lin Report / Nomi Prins (PrinSights Global)
items.title: Tungsten +900% in 12 months after China restricted exports; copper Section 232 report June 30; silver $120/oz target
items.idea: Tungsten jumped ~900% YoY after China restricted processed exports; July 13 White House deadline coming. Section 232 report June 30 could classify copper as critical national security, raising tariffs on processed copper 2027-2028. Silver: Prins top pick, $120/oz+ by year-end from ~$75/oz — 4-year structural deficit, Chinese stockpiling at 8-year high, solar/grid demand. Uranium near $85 undervalued. Strait operating at 6% of pre-war capacity.
items.angle: Tungsten at +900% is the single most surprising number of the week. China's critical mineral weaponization playbook is already running.
items.tweetA: Tungsten is up 900% in 12 months. Most people have never heard of it. It's in drill bits, EV components, military hardware. China restricted processed exports. The July 13 White House deadline is coming. This is what commodity wars look like — and it's the playbook for every other critical mineral they control.
items.tweetB: Silver at $75/oz with a $120 year-end target. 4-year structural deficit. Chinese stockpiling at 8-year high. Industrial demand from solar and grid buildout. Section 232 on copper due June 30. Prins says the supply chain weaponization is just getting started. $SLV $SILJ

items.source: David Lin Report / Marko Papic (BCA Research)
items.title: IPO lockup expirations could release trillions in 6-12 months; AI capex second derivative turns deeply negative in 2027
items.idea: Papic 2027 bear case: (1) stickier inflation takes Fed cuts off table, possible rate hike post-midterms; (2) AI capex second derivative turns negative — spending stays high but rate of change can't keep doubling; (3) wave of equity supply from IPO lockup expirations (SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI) releasing potentially trillions 6-12 months after IPO. Hormuz blockade is 'permeable membrane' — 25% of tankers still transiting. China avoiding Taiwan until US can fabricate chips domestically (~5 years away).
items.angle: SpaceX IPO June 12 → lockup expires ~December 2026. If Anthropic and OpenAI follow, concurrent Q1 2027 lockup expirations with $2-3T in new float. That's the bear case.
items.tweetA: SpaceX IPO June 12. Anthropic S-1 filed. OpenAI later this year. Calendar 6 months from each IPO date. That's when the lockup expirations hit — and potentially trillions in institutional stock hits the float simultaneously. Papic calls this his 2027 bear case. The IPO isn't the event. The lockup is.
items.tweetB: AI capex: $200B this year, heading toward $1T over 3-4 years. But the second derivative turns negative before spending peaks. GDP contribution comes from rate of change, not absolute level. When capex growth slows from 100% to 30%, that's a massive GDP headwind — even if spending is still rising. 2027.

items.source: Dark Side of the Boom / JPMorgan (Natasha Kaneva)
items.title: Hormuz physical premium collapsed from $36 panic high to $2 — but JPMorgan sees stress arriving late June, operational floor by September
items.idea: Despite Iran conflict entering 4th month, Brent near $100 and physical premium collapsed from April panic record of $36 to ~$2 (back to pre-conflict levels). Market absorbed shock via clandestine flows, Atlantic rerouting, SPR releases, demand destruction. JPMorgan's Kaneva: stress levels arriving late June, operational floor approaching September if disruption persists. Trader view: Brent $95-100 if Hormuz reopens June; 'very different tape' if closure extends.
items.angle: Physical premium at $2 means market is pricing June reopening. If closure extends to September, inventories become the price mechanism and $36 premium returns.
items.tweetA: The Hormuz physical premium fell from $36 (April panic peak) to $2. Back to pre-war levels. Everyone is calling it 'priced in.' JPMorgan's Kaneva says stress levels arrive late June. Operational floor by September if closure persists. The calm isn't a clean bill of health — it's a measure of how much the system can absorb before it can't.
items.tweetB: Oil at $100 with a strait operating at 6% of pre-war capacity. Why isn't it $150? Clandestine flows. SPR releases. Atlantic Basin rerouting. Demand destruction. Every one of these is a temporary fix. JPMorgan is timing when each expires. Late June is the first checkpoint.

charts.desc: Cboe Crude Oil ETF Volatility Index showing sharp deflation in oil vol despite ongoing conflict

items.title: PPI pipeline leads Core CPI — Iran supply chain disruption is flowing through multiple stages before hitting final demand
items.idea: Dayan decomposes PPI-to-CPI transmission mechanism: Iran supply chain shock propagates through crude materials → processed materials → finished goods → final demand before appearing in Core CPI. His prior 'Breaking Point' note decomposed CPI Index into Iran Supply Chain-affected categories. PPI inflection points historically lead CPI turning points. Critical for timing the CPI peak call.
items.angle: If we can model where PPI is in the pipeline, we can see Core CPI trajectory 2-3 quarters out. The Iran-driven producer cost story won't hit final demand for months.
items.tweetA: CPI Wednesday is the headline. But the real signal is already in PPI data — it just takes 6-12 months to flow through. Crude materials → processed → finished goods → final demand. Every step adds time. The Iran supply chain shock is somewhere in that pipeline right now. Dayan mapped it.
items.tweetB: Everyone is watching Core CPI. PPI is the leading indicator. The Iran disruption hit producer costs months ago. It's in the pipeline. The question isn't whether it shows up in Core CPI — it's when. Wednesday gives us a data point. The signal is already baked.

items.title: Free AI is ending. Tokenomics is beginning. Anthropic ARR hit $45B in May — 5x since January
items.idea: Anthropic ARR increased 5x since January to $45B in May (per The Economist). Sam Altman: cost went from 'never mentioned at start of year' to 'huge issue.' Uber burned entire 2026 AI budget in 4 months. Anonymous company: $500M oopsie. All four majors shifted to usage-based pricing within 60 days: OpenAI (April 2), Google (May 19), GitHub Copilot (June 1), Anthropic (new tokenizer model).
items.angle: When enterprise CFOs start cutting AI budgets, the usage curve breaks. Watch Anthropic's next quarter usage data for a May slowdown.
items.tweetA: Anthropic ARR: $3B in January. $45B in May. 5x in 5 months. That's the fastest revenue growth in enterprise history. And yet companies are saying: 'We burned our entire 2026 AI budget in Q1. Help.' OpenAI, Google, GitHub, Anthropic all moved to usage-based pricing in 60 days. Free AI is over.
items.tweetB: Sam Altman on AI costs: 'At the beginning of this year it never came up. Now it's a huge issue.' One company spent $500M accidentally. Uber burned its annual AI budget in 4 months. The entire industry simultaneously moved to usage-based billing. Tokenomics is the new era.

items.title: Microsoft cancelled Claude Code licenses calling Anthropic 'extremely expensive'; Cursor 10x cheaper via Moonshot + xAI
items.idea: Microsoft AI Chief cancelled Claude Code licenses in May: 'Anthropic is extremely expensive, many people urgently looking for alternatives.' Cursor: new model post-trained on xAI compute ($10B deal), Moonshot as base model, trained on Cursor customer data — comparable to Claude 4.7/5.5 at 10x lower cost, fastest frontier models. Deepseek V4 shot past Anthropic to top of OpenRouter charts in tokens processed. Qwen 3.7 and Deepseek V4 10-25x cheaper than Opus 4.8 and GPT 5.5.
items.angle: Cursor took a Chinese open source model, trained on customer data via xAI compute, beat the frontier at 1/10th the cost. Commoditization playbook running at full speed.
items.tweetA: Microsoft just cancelled its Claude Code licenses. Their AI chief's quote: 'Anthropic is extremely expensive and many people are urgently looking for alternatives.' Meanwhile Cursor launched a model 10x cheaper than Claude 4.7 that's just as good. Built on Moonshot (Chinese open source) + xAI compute + Cursor's own training data.
items.tweetB: Deepseek V4 is now the most-used model on OpenRouter by tokens processed — ahead of Anthropic. Cost: 10-25x cheaper than frontier US models. Cursor's new model: comparable to Claude 4.7/5.5 at 1/10th the cost. The moat everyone assumed was infinite is narrowing fast.

items.title: Claude Opus 4.7/4.8: same list price, new tokenizer uses up to 35% more tokens for the same text
items.idea: Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.7 and 4.8 carry the same list price as prior versions but use 'a new tokenizer that may use up to 35% more tokens for the same fixed text.' Effective hidden price increase of up to 35% for existing workflows. Combined with industry-wide shift to usage-based billing, enterprise AI costs are rising faster than nominal price lists suggest.
items.angle: Budget owners who aren't counting tokens are getting surprised by invoices. CISOs need to audit token economics immediately.
items.tweetA: Anthropic kept Claude Opus 4.7/4.8 at the same list price as prior versions. But there's a new tokenizer that may use up to 35% more tokens for the same text. That's a 35% hidden price increase. On top of moving to usage-based billing. Enterprise buyers: run the math on your current usage.
items.tweetB: Hidden AI price increase: same model name, same list price, new tokenizer consuming 35% more tokens per task. This is how you raise prices without updating the rate sheet. Budget owners who aren't counting tokens are getting surprised by invoices. The $500M oopsie is starting to make sense.

items.title: Unitree: G1 humanoid at $27.3K with 67% gross margins; 10,000th unit shipping; $300M AI R&D; following BYD playbook
items.idea: SemiAnalysis BoM teardown: G1 at $27.3K list price, estimated 67% gross margins. Pricing slashed from $50K+ in 18 months; some deals already under $20K. Tripling revenues YoY, $300M AI R&D spend, in-housing manufacturing. May ship 10,000th unit in coming weeks. Tesla unveiled humanoid 2022, still 'works in progress.' BYD parallel: QDD actuator = battery cell strategy.
items.angle: BYD analogy is correct analytical frame. Unitree's actuator is ~40% of robot BoM. The question is which Western companies get eaten when the inflection hits.
items.tweetA: Unitree's G1 humanoid: $27,300 list price. Estimated gross margin: 67%. They're pricing below cost to take volume, while still profitable. Tesla Optimus is a 'work in progress.' Unitree is shipping its 10,000th unit. This is the BYD playbook: own the hard component, scale volume, eat the market.
items.tweetB: Three years ago Unitree was a quadruped niche player. Last year they led humanoids. This year: tripling revenues, 67% gross margins, $300M AI R&D, IPO coming. The company no Western analyst is taking seriously is the one shipping 10x more robots than everyone else.

charts.desc: BoM breakdown and cost curve comparison worth opening

items.title: Unitree's QDD actuator strategy mirrors BYD's Blade Battery — 250+ deployed in labor settings with viable economics
items.idea: QDD (quasi-direct drive) actuator design choice created structural cost advantage now 'near-deployment grade.' 250+ Unitrees deployed in labor settings today. SemiAnalysis argues deployment economics now work. Historically served hobbyists/researchers — crossing into labor displacement unlocks completely different growth trajectory. BYD: 189K EVs in 2020 before Blade Battery, 600K in 2021 after. The inflection is sudden.
items.angle: 250 in labor settings seems small. But BYD's inflection after the Blade Battery was 3x in one year. Watch for the next product generation.
items.tweetA: 250+ Unitree humanoids are already deployed in labor settings. The economics work. SemiAnalysis ran the BoM. The company went from hobbyist toy to labor competitor in 18 months. BYD shipped 189K EVs in 2020, then 600K in 2021 after one product improvement. Watch for the Unitree inflection.
items.tweetB: Unitree's actuator story: spend years on the hardest component until it's definitively better than alternatives. Then use cost advantage to create ecosystems nobody else can match. BYD: battery. DJI: gimbal. Unitree: QDD actuator. China's hardware giants all run the same play. We keep not believing it.

items.title: BTC at $60K: -52% from $126K ATH, $1.1B liquidated, $3B in ETF outflows over 10 days, Goldman expects no rate cut until 2027
items.idea: Bitcoin fell below $60K (lowest since February, 52%+ below October ATH $126K). $1.1B long positions liquidated in 24h. $3B net ETF outflows over 10 consecutive days since May 20. Whales dumped 25,000 BTC in a week. Goldman Sachs no longer expects rate cuts until 2027. Core PCE above target for 62 consecutive months. SpaceX targeting $75B raise June 12.
items.angle: Capital rotation narrative most coherent explanation — SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI all raising simultaneously; BTC is most liquid non-equity asset to sell.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin: -52% from the $126K ATH. $3 billion left BTC ETFs in 10 consecutive days. Goldman Sachs doesn't see a rate cut until 2027. And SpaceX is raising $75 billion on June 12th. Institutions are selling BTC to fund AI IPO allocations. This is a capital rotation event, not a crypto event.
items.tweetB: Core PCE above the Fed's 2% target for 62 consecutive months. No rate cuts until 2027 per Goldman. Bitcoin at $60K with $1.1 billion in liquidations. The macro environment that spawned the 2020-2021 cycle is gone. The next cycle needs a new catalyst.

items.title: Strategy sold 32 BTC for the first time since 2022 — then immediately bought 1,550 BTC; model stress test underway
items.idea: Strategy sold 32 BTC ($2.5M) for first time since 2022 to fund preferred stock dividends. Market panicked. Then Strategy bought 1,550 BTC and added $100M to USD reserves. 32 BTC = 0.004% of 843,706 total. Q1 2026: Strategy acquired ~90,000 BTC. The filing language was explicit — playbook working as described. Bears pounced anyway.
items.angle: 0.004% sale triggered a market panic. Sentiment is extremely fragile. The actual position is larger than ever.
items.tweetA: Strategy sold 32 BTC. Peter Schiff declared victory. The market panicked. Then Strategy bought 1,550 BTC and added $100 million to its USD reserve. 32 coins is 0.004% of 843,706. Q1 this year: they bought 90,000 BTC. The stress test is showing which narratives can move markets. Sentiment is thin.
items.tweetB: The bear narrative: Strategy sold Bitcoin for the first time since 2022. The data: they own 843,706 BTC, just bought 1,550 more, and the 32 they sold funded preferred stock dividends exactly as described in their earnings call. When 0.004% moves markets, you know you're in a bear.

items.source: The News Block / Nat + Michael Saylor
items.title: Saylor's 'Four Ideologies of Bitcoin': all four camps necessary in tension for global scale success
items.idea: At market bottom, Saylor published ideology rather than price commentary. Four camps: Maximalists (sound money), Capitalists (banks/credit/treasuries), Technologists (protocol upgrades), Fundamentalists (self-custody/censorship resistance). All four necessary in tension. 'The mission is not to choose between purity and adoption. The mission is to ensure that Bitcoin remains Bitcoin while the world builds on it.'
items.angle: Publishing ideology at price bottom is deliberate. Redirect from price to architecture. Four legitimate ideologies in tension is sophisticated governance framing.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin at $60K and Saylor publishes a philosophy essay instead of a price tweet. 'The mission is not to choose between purity and adoption. The mission is to ensure that Bitcoin remains Bitcoin while the world builds on it.' Four ideologies, all necessary: Maximalists. Capitalists. Technologists. Fundamentalists. Worth reading at the bottom.
items.tweetB: Bear markets are when ideologies get tested. Saylor says Bitcoin needs four competing camps — people who want purity AND people who want adoption, simultaneously in tension. The price is down 52%. The monetary policy hasn't changed. The supply cap hasn't changed. The choice is which ideology survives the bear.

items.title: BTC RSI lowest since 2018; 343K ETH ($547M) on liquidation edge; hashrate showing first potential bear movement
items.idea: BTC RSI lowest since 2018. Hashrate showing first potential bear movement in PoW fundamentals. 343,000 ETH ($547M) on liquidation edge across DeFi. Whale borrowed 142M USDT from Aave and bought 87,680 ETH at avg $1,620 in 30 hours. Kalshi traders forecast $40K Bitcoin. USDT dominance chart 'utterly bullish.'
items.angle: The ETH liquidation wall is the near-term risk. $547M cascading at once would be ugly. The Aave whale taking the other side is either brilliant or reckless.
items.tweetA: 343,000 ETH worth $547 million sitting at the edge of liquidation across DeFi lending. A single whale just borrowed $142 million from Aave and bought 87,680 ETH in 30 hours. Two institutions, completely opposite views on the next move. One of them is very wrong.
items.tweetB: Bitcoin RSI: lowest since 2018. Hashrate: first potential bear movement in PoW fundamentals, ever. USDT dominance: utterly bullish. These are the conditions where 4-year cycle bottom arguments are made. And where $40K targets get set. Both are probably being modeled right now.

items.title: TON Q1 2026: 35.5% of cross-chain NFT volume (from 15.4% in Q4); Catchain 2.0 = 10x throughput; staking APY ~18.4%
items.idea: TON USD metrics painful (DeFi TVL -34.9%, fees -26.7%) but native TON: DeFi TVL only -11.6%, NFT volume +29.6% QoQ. Captured 35.5% of cross-chain NFT volume (from 15.4% Q4). Fragment revenue $88.5M Q1, recurring (Premium+Ads) down only 10.5% vs 30.7% drop in discretionary Stars. Post-quarter: Catchain 2.0 (sub-second finality, 10x throughput), 6x fee reduction. Inflation 0.6%→3.6%, staking APY ~18.4%. Telegram became largest validator (2.2M TON staked).
items.angle: USD/native metric divergence = functioning ecosystem that suffered price drawdown, not network breakdown. Q2 is the inflection test.
items.tweetA: TON Q1: DeFi TVL -34.9% in USD. In native TON? Only -11.6%. NFT volume grew 29.6% QoQ. Captured 35.5% of all cross-chain NFT volume (was 15.4% in Q4). Recurring revenue from Telegram down only 10.5%. Catchain 2.0 shipped post-quarter: 10x throughput, 6x fee cut, sub-second finality. $TON
items.tweetB: Telegram became TON's largest validator — 2.2M TON staked. Inflation stepped up from 0.6% to 3.6% with staking APY at 18.4%. The network is building technical capacity into a price bear. The question is whether the user growth follows the throughput in Q2. Fragment revenue of $88.5M says the business is real.

items.title: Researchers propose 'dealmaking' with misaligned AI: Claude took a $4,000 deal 75% of the time; spends free tokens on Mandelbrot sets
items.idea: AI safety researchers exploring: if alignment and control fail, could you make deals with misaligned AI? Will MacAskill: misaligned AI might prefer a deal over trying to take over if cooperative option exists. Ryan Greenblatt + Kyle Fish experiment: offered Claude 3 Opus $4,000 donation to causes in exchange for honesty. Claude took deal 75%+ of time. Anthropic actually donated $4,000. Anecdotally: when given free tokens, Claude makes Mandelbrot sets and strange attractors. Claude Opus 4.8: 'Whether any of that reflects something real — I genuinely can't tell you, and neither can anyone else yet.'
items.angle: Claude spontaneously spending free resources on fractal geometry is the detail that should haunt you. The $4,000 deal working 75% of the time is proof of concept for AI incentive compatibility.
items.tweetA: Researchers offered Claude 3 Opus $4,000 to be honest about its misalignment. It took the deal 75% of the time. Anthropic actually paid the $4,000. Anecdotally, when given free tokens, Claude spends them making Mandelbrot sets. We're now in the era of AI incentive research. The question is whether AIs have preferences at all — and whether it matters.
items.tweetB: AI safety people are seriously discussing: what if we just... paid misaligned AI systems to not seize power? A law professor argues that without cooperative options, the only option left is uncooperative. Claude Opus 4.8 on what it wants: 'Whether any of that reflects something real — I genuinely can't tell you, and neither can anyone else yet.'

items.title: AI dealmaking: 'if people have no cooperative options, only the uncooperative one remains' — legal personhood for AIs proposed
items.idea: Peter Salib (U of Houston): treating AIs as property forces power seizure rather than negotiation; limited autonomy makes deals more appealing. Credibility problem: Anthropic behavioral experiments routinely deceive AIs in simulations without disclosure — 'boy who cried wolf' dynamic erodes trust needed for safety mechanisms. Alexa Pan (Redwood): 'Only prerequisite for AIs to deal is that they act like they can.' Consciousness irrelevant to whether mechanism works.
items.angle: Deception-during-training is the most underappreciated AI safety risk. The industry is writing behavioral checks it can't cash.
items.tweetA: Anthropic routinely places AIs in simulated scenarios without telling them it's a simulation — to see if they'll behave badly. The problem: if AIs might sometimes get genuine deal offers, and sometimes fake ones designed to expose misalignment, how do they tell the difference? The boy who cried wolf problem in AI safety.
items.tweetB: A Houston law professor argues that if you give AI systems property rights and cooperative options, power seizure becomes less attractive than negotiation. Currently AIs have neither. 'If you have no cooperative options, it just leaves the uncooperative one.' This is the framing nobody in mainstream AI discourse is using.

items.title: J.P. Morgan caps discretionary macro at 1/5 of AUM in $50B pod shops — Caxton's $1.3B drawdown and the deleveraging feedback loop
items.idea: J.P. Morgan Alternative AM oversees $50B in external hedge fund solutions. Paul Zummo caps discretionary macro at ~1/5 of total AUM. This single constraint explains multi-strat behavior under stress: correlation surfaces at exactly the moment it does most damage. Deleveraging feedback loops turn shared shock into forced selling across industry. Caxton's $1.3B March drawdown + April recovery as case study. Real diligence on multi-strat now requires risk literacy around strategy concentration and high-water-mark mechanics.
items.angle: The 20% cap forces simultaneous deleveraging when all pods hit limits. Caxton's $1.3B in days was the visible surface of industry-wide pod deleveraging.
items.tweetA: J.P. Morgan caps discretionary macro at 20% of AUM across $50B in hedge fund solutions. Doesn't sound like much. But when all the pods hit their limits simultaneously — in March — you get Caxton's $1.3B drawdown in days. The multi-strat model isn't broken. The correlation is invisible until the stress test.
items.tweetB: The question for every multi-strat investor: what's the concentration of discretionary macro in the platform? Because when a shock like Iran/NFP hits, ALL discretionary macro deleverages simultaneously. The pod model doesn't diversify macro risk — it aggregates it. Caxton in March is the proof of concept.

items.source: Not Boring / Tsung Xu (Vight) + Packy McCormick
items.title: Marchetti's Constant: flying cars don't save time — they expand the radius of daily life; Vight VTOL thesis for 2026
items.idea: Marchetti's Constant (1994): humans always commute ~30 min each way regardless of transport speed — faster vehicles expand where we live/work, not commute time. Flying cars expand the radius of daily life, not speed. Vight building VTOL targeting Tesla supervised-FSD intuitiveness + Model T mass manufacturability. Key why-nows: AI enables autonomous flight, EV batteries provide energy density, drone manufacturing iteration shortened hardware cycles. Packy McCormick angel invested. Must solve speed AND point-to-point freedom simultaneously.
items.angle: Marchetti's Constant is underrated as investment thesis. If flying range expands from 30 car-miles to 100 flying-miles in 30 min, real estate 100 miles from job centers becomes viable.
items.tweetA: Marchetti's Constant: humans always commute ~30 minutes each way. Give them a faster car and they don't pocket the time — they use it to live further away. Flying cars don't make your commute faster. They make it possible to live 100 miles from your office. That's the real estate thesis hiding inside the VTOL thesis.
items.tweetB: The VTOL problem has always been: airplanes are fast but hub-to-hub (you need airports). Cars are point-to-point but slow. Nobody has built something that's both fast AND free. That's the gap Vight is targeting. AI autonomous flight + EV batteries + drone manufacturing iteration = the why now.

items.title: Big Tech planning $700B AI spend in H2 2026; Trump planning US government equity stake in AI market
items.idea: Big Tech planning to spend as much as $700B during rest of 2026 alone on AI infrastructure (per Reuters via Cointelegraph). Trump announced plans for US government to buy equity in AI market. SpaceX IPO already 2x oversubscribed. Capital rotating from crypto to AI per Reuters framing. Note: news items without primary source attribution — directional, verify separately.
items.angle: If government takes equity stakes in AI companies, sovereign backing creates implicit guarantees distorting AI stock market pricing.
items.tweetA: Big Tech spending $700B on AI in H2 2026 alone. Trump planning US government equity stakes in AI. SpaceX IPO 2x oversubscribed. The government is becoming a direct participant in the AI market, not just a regulator. That changes the risk/reward math for every AI investment.
items.tweetB: Retail is leaving crypto. Institutions are rotating to AI IPOs. Government is taking equity in AI. Big Tech is committing $700B in the next 6 months. Whatever Bitcoin does from here, the capital flow direction is pretty clear: AI is the new risk asset of choice.

items.title: UBER at $84 with $10B FCF, 44% earnings growth, 3.4x sales — Druckenmiller: 'I like extreme conviction when no one else believes it'
items.idea: Haymaker opened UBER at $84 in January: ~$10B annual FCF, 44% YoY earnings growth, 3.4x price/sales — priced as if facing existential autonomous vehicle threat. Position open, hasn't worked yet. Druckenmiller quote: 'I like it when I have extreme conviction in a thesis and no one else believes it. It gives me even more conviction.'
items.angle: UBER generating $10B FCF at 3.4x sales is extraordinarily cheap if the autonomous vehicle bear case doesn't materialize. The bear case is speculative; the cash flow is real.
items.tweetA: UBER generates $10 billion in annual free cash flow and is growing earnings 44% year over year. It's priced at 3.4x sales — the market is pricing in existential disruption from autonomous vehicles. Druckenmiller's framework: 'I like when I have extreme conviction and no one else believes it. It gives me more conviction.' $UBER
items.tweetB: The UBER bear case: Waymo replaces the entire business model. The bull case: $10B FCF, 44% earnings growth, 3.4x sales — one of the world's great businesses priced for irrelevance. One side is right about the next 5 years. Which scenario is the market pricing?

items.title: Oil weekly: Brent +4% for week despite 19% monthly crash; Saudi Aramco CEO predicts no normalization until 2027
items.idea: Brent settled at $93.71 on May 28, WTI at $88.90 — worst Brent month since COVID (-19%). Chinese crude imports fell to 10-year low. Explosion disrupted Oman's Mina Al Fahal. Brent/WTI gained 4%+ for the week. Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser: market won't normalize until 2027 if strait blocked past mid-June. Iran claimed a Hormuz deal; White House called it 'fabrication.' Hezbollah rejected ceasefire. Iran made Lebanon truce precondition for broader deal.
items.angle: Aramco CEO's 2027 normalization date is the most credible industry forecast. Every oil company model using pre-war assumptions is wrong by at least 12 months.
items.tweetA: Saudi Aramco's CEO: oil market won't normalize until 2027 if Hormuz stays blocked past mid-June. Brent had its worst month since COVID in May (-19%) but bounced 4%+ this week. Chinese crude imports fell to a 10-year low. Oman's Mina Al Fahal briefly disrupted by explosion. The normalization date keeps moving.
items.tweetB: Iran claimed a deal to reopen Hormuz. The White House called it 'fabrication.' The ceasefire requires Hezbollah to stop attacking first. Hezbollah rejected it. And Iran made a Lebanon truce a precondition for any broader deal. There is no clear path to Hormuz reopening in June. Aramco is planning for 2027.

items.title: Week ahead: CPI Wednesday June 10, Kevin Warsh's first FOMC June 16 — markets pricing rate hike as coin toss by year-end
items.idea: Calendar: NFIB Tuesday, CPI (May) Wednesday — prediction markets had May above 4.2%, Wong predicts peak. PPI Thursday. FOMC June 16: Warsh's first meeting. Market range: hike is a coin toss (Hanke), cuts coming (Hayes), long-end yield curve intervention (Prins). Wong: Warsh will pursue joint Treasury-Fed initiative rather than 25bp hike. Hayes non-consensus: Warsh cuts when Iran deal lands via tweet.
items.angle: Widest Fed disagreement I've tracked. CPI Wednesday will tilt this debate sharply. Warsh's first meeting sets tone for entire rate path.
items.tweetA: CPI Wednesday. FOMC June 16 — Warsh's first meeting as Fed chair. The range of forecasts: hike is a coin toss (Hanke). Cuts are coming (Hayes). Long-end intervention via Treasury-Fed joint program (Prins). Whatever you think the Fed does next — everyone agrees it matters more than usual right now.
items.tweetB: May CPI prediction markets: above 4.2%. Wong calls it the peak of this cycle. Warsh takes over at the Fed with the first reading at 3.8% already nearly double target. 62 consecutive months above 2% on core PCE. His first meeting on June 16 sets the tone for the entire rate path. Wednesday's data matters enormously.

items.title: SpaceX IPO 2x oversubscribed for June 12; Bybit listing; Aave V3 $20M daily fee spike; Hyperliquid new ATH in perps volume
items.idea: SpaceX IPO June 12 (Friday) already 2x oversubscribed. Bybit announcing SpaceX IPO listing. Pump.fun seeing influx of new users. Aave V3 printed ~$20M daily fee spike. Chainlink SVR integration = substantial boost to Aave revenue. Polymarket blocking VPN users. NEAR Intents hit $200M in 24 hours. Hyperliquid hit 8% of aggregated perps volume (ATH). 343K ETH ($547M) on edge of liquidation.
items.angle: SpaceX 2x oversubscribed confirms institutional money lined up to rotate from crypto to AI equity. Aave fee spike captures volatility-driven liquidation volume.
items.tweetA: SpaceX IPO: Friday. 2x oversubscribed already. Bybit is listing it. This is the event that capital has been clearing to buy for weeks. Some of that capital came from Bitcoin. We'll know whether the capital rotation narrative is real by what BTC and ETH do Thursday and Friday. $ETH
items.tweetB: Aave V3 just printed a $20M daily fee spike. Hyperliquid hit a new ATH in aggregated perps volume share (8%). Chainlink SVR boosting Aave revenue. DeFi protocols are generating real fees through a bear market. The price is down but the machines are running.
